description:
This diagram illustrates the core principles of Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) for monitoring ground motion using satellite Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) data.

Part (a) depicts the InSAR (Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar) sensing geometry: Two satellite passes are shown, with radar signals R1 (blue) and R2 (red) transmitted and received from a ground target, specifically a building identified as a Persistent Scatterer (PS). The difference in range, ΔR, highlights a change in position or path length between the two acquisitions. Trees marked with red crosses indicate non-persistent scatterers.

Part (b) presents a 3D data stack, where multiple layers of radar data are shown across "slant range" (X-axis, 0 to 100 units), "azimuth" (Y-axis, 0 to 100 units), and "baseline" (Z-axis, 0 to 10 units). Several points are marked: a "Ref" (Reference) point and multiple "PS" (Persistent Scatterer) points, with vertical lines extending through the stacked layers. The layers exhibit varying signal intensities, represented by a colour gradient from blue (lower) to red (higher).

Part (c) illustrates the time series analysis for selected points in "space" over "time" (from t0 to tN). Horizontal lines indicate different acquisition times, with corresponding phase or displacement values α0 through αN. Three points are analyzed: a PS (left), a Ref (center), and another PS (right). For the left PS, the signal (blue undulating line) shows phase changes over time, indicated by red shaded areas and a red downward arrow, suggesting cumulative deformation. The Ref point's signal remains stable over time. For the right PS, the signal shows different phase changes with blue shaded areas and a blue downward arrow, indicating cumulative deformation distinct from the left PS. This part demonstrates the detection of relative ground motion for Persistent Scatterers by tracking coherent phase changes over multiple acquisitions.
